Move's resource types guarantee that assets cannot be copied or accidentally destroyed — enforced at the bytecode level.
Sui Move uses an object-centric model where every on-chain value is an object with a unique UID.
Programmable Transaction Blocks (PTBs) allow chaining up to 1024 Move calls in a single atomic transaction.
The `has key` ability means an object can be transferred between addresses and stored in the global state.
The `has store` ability allows a struct to be stored inside another struct that has `key`.
The `has copy` ability allows a value to be duplicated — used for primitive types and small value types.
The `has drop` ability allows a value to be discarded without explicit destruction.
Shared objects require consensus in Sui, while owned objects can be processed in parallel without consensus.
Dynamic fields allow Sui objects to grow unboundedly at runtime — essential for collections and maps.
The `transfer::share_object` function makes an object accessible to all users simultaneously.
Move's borrow checker prevents dangling references — all references must outlive their referents.
Generic functions in Move allow writing reusable code across different coin types: `fun swap<T>(...)`.
The Sui Framework provides `coin::Coin<T>` as the standard fungible token primitive.
Events in Sui Move are emitted using `event::emit(EventStruct { ... })` and indexed by Sui nodes.
Test scenarios in Move use `sui::test_scenario` to simulate multi-transaction test flows.
The `#[test_only]` attribute gates functions to testing builds only — crucial for init_for_testing patterns.
Move structs cannot have recursive types — all cyclic data must use object references (IDs).
The `object::id(obj)` function returns the ID of any object with the `key` ability.
Sui's clock object (0x6) provides on-chain timestamps — always pass `&Clock` rather than using block data.
Hot potato pattern in Move: a struct with no abilities forces callers to consume it within the same PTB.
Witness pattern: a struct with `drop` proves ownership of a module — used for one-time initializations.
Publisher pattern: `package::claim<OTW>` in `init` grants module publisher rights for standard compliance.
The `TxContext` provides `sender()`, `epoch()`, and `fresh_object_address()` in Move functions.
Move 2024 edition introduced `let else` pattern, improved match syntax, and module-level `use` statements.
Kiosk standard enables NFT and object marketplaces with programmable transfer policies and royalties.
Display standard lets objects define how they appear in wallets and explorers via key-value metadata.
The `sui::table` module provides a map-like structure that counts toward object storage.
VecMap and VecSet are O(n) collections suitable for small datasets — use Table for larger ones.
Move's type system prevents unauthorized coin minting: only the module that defines a coin can mint it.
Package upgrades in Sui require UpgradeCap and are restricted to additive changes (no breaking interfaces).
Move unit tests run in milliseconds — they execute against a simulated Sui runtime, not a full node.
The `coin::split` and `coin::merge` functions are the standard way to handle partial coin payments.
Move's entry functions are restricted in what types they can accept from user input — no arbitrary references.
Epoch in Sui represents a ~24 hour period — used for staking rewards, validator rotation, and time-locks.
The `balance::Balance<T>` type represents a token amount without transfer metadata — used inside objects.
Object transfer via `transfer::public_transfer` requires the `key + store` abilities on the object.
Move's formal verifier (the Move Prover) can prove absence of arithmetic overflow and invariant violations.
Sui's object versioning: every write increments `version` — used for optimistic locking in concurrent access.
The `UID` type wraps `object::ID` and is the root of every Sui object's identity.
Capability objects in Move grant powers: holding a `MinterCap` allows minting, `AdminCap` allows admin ops.
